elements.sent-2411-001: We consider cylindrical algebraic decompositions (CADs) as a tool for representing semi-algebraic subsets of $\mathbb{R ^n$.}
elements.sent-2411-002: In this framework, a CAD $\mathscr{C}$ is adapted to a given set $S$ if $S$ is a union of cells of $\mathscr{C}$.
elements.sent-2411-003: Different algorithms computing an adapted CAD may produce different outputs, usually with redundant cell divisions.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper we analyse the possibility to remove the superfluous data.
elements.sent-2411-005: More precisely we consider the set CAD$(S)$ of CADs that are adapted to $S$, endowed with the refinement partial order and we study the existence of minimal and minimum elements in this poset.
elements.sent-2411-006: We show that for every semi-algebraic set $S$ of $\mathbb{R ^n$ and every CAD $\mathscr{C}$ adapted to $S$, there is a minimal CAD adapted to $S$ and smaller (i.e. coarser) than or equal to $\mathscr{C}$.}
elements.sent-2411-007: Moreover, when $n=1$ or $n=2$, we strengthen this result by proving the existence of a minimum element in CAD$(S)$.
elements.sent-2411-008: Astonishingly for $n \geq 3$, there exist semi-algebraic sets whose associated poset of adapted CADs does not admit a minimum.
elements.sent-2411-009: We prove this result by providing explicit examples.
elements.sent-2411-010: We finally use a reduction relation on CAD$(S)$ to define an algorithm for the computation of minimal CADs.
elements.sent-2411-011: We conclude with a characterization of those semi-algebraic sets $S$ for which CAD$(S)$ has a minimum by means of confluence of the associated reduction system.
elements.sent-2411-012: Cylindrical algebraic decomposition (CAD) is a fundamental tool in computer algebra and in real algebraic geometry.
elements.sent-2411-013: Since its introduction by Collins \cite{collins1975 in the framework of real quantifier elimination, the scope of application of CAD and the concept itself have evolved in various directions.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Algorithms computing CADs are constantly refined \cite{LazardStyle23, McCallum98, Brown01, RegularChains and are nowadays used as building blocks in various fields where symbolic manipulations of polynomial equalities and inequalities are relevant.}
elements.sent-2411-015: From a theoretical standpoint, the existence of a CAD algorithm may also be seen as a structure theorem for semi-algebraic sets not only giving topological information about them (see for example \cite{bochnaketal1998, schwartzsharir83,basu2007 ) but also providing a constructive and explicit representation of these subsets enabling symbolic and effective reasoning.}
elements.sent-2411-016: In this framework, a CAD is said to be adapted to (or represents) a semi-algebraic set $S \subset \mathbb{R ^n$ if $S$ is a union of cells from the CAD.}
elements.sent-2411-017: In practice, for computing a CAD adapted to a semi-algebraic set $S$, one describes $S$ by polynomial inequalities (conjunction, disjunction, and negation) associated with polynomials in a finite set $\mathcal{F \subset \mathbb{Z [x_1,\ldots,x_n]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: The CAD algorithms usually take $\mathcal{F}$ as input and the output CAD is therefore adapted to any semi-algebraic set defined by means of $\mathcal{F}$.
elements.sent-2411-019: For this reason they usually contain unnecessary cells for the description of $S$.
elements.sent-2411-020: Moreover, these outputs also depend on the choice of $\mathcal{F}$ and on the considered algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-021: It is then natural to seek for a method for simplifying a given CAD, while retaining only the relevant information needed to describe $S$.
elements.sent-2411-022: More precisely, we study in detail the set CAD$(S)$ of CADs (with respect to a fixed variable ordering) that are adapted to $S$.
elements.sent-2411-023: This set is naturally endowed with the partial order $\preceq$ defined by refinement: for $\mathscr{C , \mathscr{C ' \in \text{CAD (S)$, we write $\mathscr{C \preceq \mathscr{C '$ if every cell of $\mathscr{C}$ is a union of cells of $\mathscr{C '$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Here, the main problems\footnote{These problems were already mentioned in the same framework in \cite{wilson,locatelli .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: These problems, theoretical in nature, lead us to analyse in detail the more practical question of simplifying CADs by merging cells (see Section \ref{sec:red ).}
elements.sent-2411-026: Finally, the minimum number of cells of these minimal CADs provide a tight lower bound on the number of cells that a CAD adapted to a semi-algebraic set must contain and could therefore be used to benchmark CAD algorithms (see for example \cite{NuCAD, MLCAD ).}
elements.sent-2411-027: In Section \ref{sec:minimal , we develop the first results on minimal CADs.}
elements.sent-2411-028: We show in particular that for every semi-algebraic set $S$ of $\mathbb{R ^n$ and every CAD $\mathscr{C}$ adapted to $S$, there is a minimal CAD adapted to $S$ and smaller (i.e. coarser) than or equal to $\mathscr{C}$.}
elements.sent-2411-029: This elementary result settles the question of the existence of minimal CADs adapted to $S$.
elements.sent-2411-030: Section \ref{sec:minimum proposes a thorough investigation of minimum CADs.}
elements.sent-2411-031: If $n=1$ or $n=2$, we prove the existence of a minimum element in CAD$(S)$ for every semi-algebraic $S$ of $\mathbb{R ^n$.}
elements.sent-2411-032: We actually use results concerning minimal CADs obtained in Section \ref{sec:minimal to show that such CADs must coincide.}
elements.sent-2411-033: On the contrary, for $n \geq 3$, we show that there exist semi-algebraic sets whose associated poset of adapted CADs does not admit a minimum by providing explicit examples.
elements.sent-2411-034: In Section \ref{sec:red , we study a reduction relation on the poset $\text{CAD (S)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: We first use it to define an algorithm that constructs a minimal CAD adapted to a given semi-algebraic set $S$ and smaller than or equal to a given CAD $\mathscr{C}$.
elements.sent-2411-036: This algorithm may be used as a post-processing operation of any CAD algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-037: Finally, we employ this relation to characterize those semi-algebraic sets $S$ for which there exists a minimum adapted CAD.
elements.sent-2411-038: More precisely, we prove that $\text{CAD (S)$ admits a minimum if and only if the associated reduction system is confluent.}
elements.sent-2411-039: We consider a positive integer $n \in \mathbb{N ^*$ and CADs of $\mathbb{R ^n$ with respect to a fixed variable ordering.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Since the cells of such CADs are defined inductively from cells of CADs of $\mathbb{R ^k$ ($k \leq n$) which are indexed by $k$-tuples, it is convenient to make tuple notation more concise.}
elements.sent-2411-041: We identify the $k$-tuple $I=(i_1,\ldots,i_k)\in\mathbb{N ^k$ with the corresponding word $i_1\ldots i_k$.}
elements.sent-2411-043: We denote by $\varepsilon$ the empty tuple, which corresponds to the empty word.
elements.sent-2411-044: For $j\in \mathbb{N}$ we denote by $I:j$ the $k+1$ tuple $(i_1,\ldots,i_k,j)$.
elements.sent-2411-045: See also Proposition \ref{ex:trousers1 for explicit examples of such CADs.}
elements.sent-2411-046: We say that the element $C_{I}$ of $\mathscr{C _k$ is a cell of index $I$.}
elements.sent-2411-048: When the context is clear, we identify $\mathscr{C}$ with $\mathscr{C _n$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: We usually denote by $S$ a semi-algebraic set of $\mathbb{R ^n$ and we denote by $S^c$ its complement in $\mathbb{R ^n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: We say that a CAD $\mathscr{C}$ is adapted to $S$ if $S$ is a union of cells of $\mathscr{C}$.
elements.sent-2411-051: We denote by $\text{CAD (S)$ the set of all CADs adapted to~$S$.}
elements.sent-2411-053: A direct consequence of Collins' seminal theorem \cite{collins1975 tells us that if $S_1,\ldots,S_p \subset \mathbb{R ^n$ are semi-algebraic sets, then there exists a CAD adapted to all of them simultaneously, i.e. $\text{CAD (S_1) \cap \ldots \cap \text{CAD (S_p)$ is never empty.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Since CADs are partitions, we naturally use the refinement order defined on the set of partitions to compare CADs.
elements.sent-2411-055: Let $\mathscr{C}$ and $\mathscr{C '$ be two CADs of $\mathbb{R ^n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: We say that $\mathscr{C '$ is a refinement of $\mathscr{C}$ if every cell of $\mathscr{C}$ is a union of cells of $\mathscr{C '$.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: We also say that $\mathscr{C}$ is smaller than or equal to $\mathscr{C '$ and write $\mathscr{C \preceq \mathscr{C '$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: This definition is already used in \cite{BrownSimple , where $\mathscr{C}$ is said to be simpler than $\mathscr{C '$ when $\mathscr{C \prec \mathscr{C '$ and where algorithms to compute simple CADs with respect to this order are devised.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: We also recall the definition of minimal and minimum elements.
elements.sent-2411-060: A minimal CAD adapted to $S$ is a minimal element of ($\text{CAD (S),\preceq$).}
elements.sent-2411-061: In other words, $\mathscr{C \in \text{CAD (S)$ is a minimal CAD adapted to $S$ if \begin{equation}\forall \mathscr{C}' \in CAD(S), (\mathscr{C}'\preceq \mathscr{C}) \implies (\mathscr{C}' = \mathscr{C}).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: A minimum CAD adapted to $S$ is a minimum element of ($\text{CAD (S),\preceq$).}
elements.sent-2411-063: Namely, $\mathscr{C \in \text{CAD (S)$ is a minimum CAD adapted to $S$ if \begin{equation}\forall \mathscr{C}' \in CAD(S), \mathscr{C}'\succeq \mathscr{C}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: We say that $S$ admits a minimum CAD if there exists a minimum CAD adapted to $S$.
elements.sent-2411-065: In this section we show the existence of minimal CAD adapted to any semi-algebraic set $S$.
elements.sent-2411-066: Then, we show the equivalence between the uniqueness of a minimal CAD and the existence of a minimum CAD.
elements.sent-2411-067: Finally, we give uniqueness results concerning the set of sections of the last level of minimal CADs.
elements.sent-2411-068: If $\mathscr{C}$ is a CAD adapted to $S$, then there exists a minimal CAD adapted to $S$ that is smaller than or equal to $\mathscr{C}$.
elements.sent-2411-069: The set $\text{SSP (\mathscr{C )$ of partitions that are strictly smaller than $\mathscr{C}$ is a finite poset, by definition.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: We can find a minimal element by inspection: if $\mathscr{C}$ is not a minimal element in $\text{CAD (S)$, we can find $\mathscr{C '$ in $\text{CAD (S)$ such that $\mathscr{C '\prec \mathscr{C}$, and iterate the procedure with $\mathscr{C '$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: This will end in a finite number of steps because $\text{SSP (\mathscr{C )$ is finite and strictly contains $\text{SSP (\mathscr{C ')$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Furthermore, this procedure will obviously result in a minimal CAD with the required properties.
elements.sent-2411-073: The determination of a minimal element in $\text{CAD (S)$ presented in the proof of Proposition \ref{prop:existMin is in general not efficient.}}
elements.sent-2411-074: If $\mathscr{C}$ contains $K$ cells, then the set SSP$(\mathscr{C )$ of partitions that are strictly smaller than $\mathscr{C}$ contains $B_K - 1$ elements, where $B_K$ is the $K^\text{th}$ Bell number.}

elements.sent-2411-165: As already mentioned in Remark \ref{rem:trousersHard , the determination of CADs that are adapted to a set $S$ and smaller than a given CAD $\mathscr{C}$ by direct inspection is certainly inefficient and already cumbersome even for low dimensional examples (see Section \ref{sec:minimum ).}}

elements.sent-2411-169: The tree associated with a given CAD $\mathscr{C}$ can be seen as a simplified version of $\mathscr{C}$, which only encodes its combinatorial structure.
elements.sent-2411-170: Roughly speaking, this is done by considering only the cell indices of the considered CAD and a function that encodes the inclusion of the cells in the set $S$ under consideration.
elements.sent-2411-171: We first introduce the general notion of a CAD tree.
elements.sent-2411-172: A CAD tree of depth $n$ is a pair $\mathcal{T = (T,L)$ where.}
elements.sent-2411-173: The root is labelled by the empty tuple $\varepsilon$ and if $I$ is the label of a node, then either $I$ is an $n$-tuple and the node is a leaf, or there exists $u_I \in \mathbb{N}$ such that the node has exactly $2u_I + 1$ sons labelled by $I : j$ with $j \in \{1,\ldots,2u_I + 1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-174: As we continue, we identify a node with its label, thus seeing $T$ as a subset of $\bigcup_{k=0 ^n(\mathbb{N ^*)^k$ endowed with the prefix relation.}}
elements.sent-2411-175: The redundancy in the definition above is useful for writing \eqref{eqn:L below easily.}
elements.sent-2411-176: However, it is clear that the function $L$ is completely defined by its values on the leaves of $T$.
elements.sent-2411-177: For every $\mathscr{C \in \text{CAD (S)$, the CAD tree associated with $(\mathscr{C , S)$ is the CAD tree of depth $n$ where the nodes of $T$ are the indices of the cells of $\mathscr{C}$ and where the function $L$ is defined on the leaves of $T$ by $L(I) = 1$ if $C_I \subset S$ and by $L(I) = 0$ if $C_I \subset S^c$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-178: We denote it by Tree$(\mathscr{C ,S)$ or Tree$(\mathscr{C )$ for short.}}

elements.sent-2411-191: It is obtained by removing subtrees with prefixes 4 and 5 and properly relabelling the other nodes (by subtracting $2$ to the 1-prefixes of subtrees with prefixes 6 and 7).
elements.sent-2411-192: In order perform these operations in the general case, we make use of the $k^\text{th}$ prefix map \begin{equation}p_{k} \colon \bigcup_{l=0}^{+\infty}(N^*)^l \to \bigcup_{l=0}^{+\infty}(N^*)^l \colon (i_1,\ldots,i_l) \mapsto  (i_1,\ldots,i_l)  if $l < k$, (i_1,\ldots,i_k)  if $l\geq k$. \end{equation} When we merge a section with (even) index $A$ with two surrounding sectors in a level of a CAD, we have to merge the subtrees corresponding to these cells and relabel the whole tree accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-193: This is the purpose of the functions we introduce in the next definition.
elements.sent-2411-194: For every even tuple $A \in (\mathbb{N ^*)^k$ ($k \geq 1$), we define { \begin{equation}\psi_A \colon \bigcup_{l=0}^{+\infty}(N^*)^l \to \bigcup_{l=0}^{+\infty}(N^*)^l \colon I \mapsto  I - e_k  if  p_k(I) = A, I - 2 e_k  if  \exists m \in N^* : p_k(I) = A+me_k, I  otherwise, \end{equation} where $e_k$ is the $k^\text{th}$ unit vector of $\mathbb{R ^l$ for $l \geq k$.}}}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \caption{Minimal($\mathscr{C}, S$)} \label{algo:Min} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require $\mathscr{C} \in \text{CAD}(S)$ \Ensure A minimal CAD $\mathcal{M}$ adapted to $S$ such that $\mathcal{M} \preceq \mathscr{C}$ \State Construct the set Red(Tree($\mathscr{C}$)) of reduction rules from Tree($\mathscr{C}$) \If{$\exists \Psi \in \text{Red(Tree}(\mathscr{C}))$ s.t. $\Psi$ lifts to a CAD($S$) reduction rule $\Phi$ from $\mathscr{C}$ to $\mathscr{C}'$} \State \Return Minimal($\mathscr{C}', S$) \Else{} \State \Return $\mathscr{C}$ \EndIf \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-252: These CADs can actually be obtained by reductions of a common CAD $\overline{\mathscr{C \in \text{CAD (\mathbb{T )$ which is depicted at the top of Figure \ref{fig:trousers3 while $\mathscr{C}$ and $\mathscr{C '$ are at the bottom.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-253: We thus have $\mathscr{C \stackrel{* {\leftarrow \overline{\mathscr{C \stackrel{* {\to \mathscr{C '$ but in view of Theorem~\ref{prop:lien-red-ordre , $\mathscr{C}$ and $\mathscr{C '$ cannot be reduced to a common CAD.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-254: This observation relates the existence of minimum element to the confluence property of $\text{CAD (\mathbb{T )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-255: We now show that this is a general phenomenon.
elements.sent-2411-256: We consider $\text{CAD (S)$ as the sets of objects of an abstract reduction system (see \cite{TRaAT ) endowed with the reduction relation introduced in Definition \ref{def:CADRed .}}}
elements.sent-2411-257: Confluence, also known as Church-Rosser or diamond property, appears in various areas of mathematics and computer science.
elements.sent-2411-258: We recall its definition in this particular case.
elements.sent-2411-259: The reduction system $\text{CAD (S)$ is globally confluent if for all $\mathscr{C , \overline{\mathscr{C , \mathscr{C ' \in \text{CAD (S)$ such that $\mathscr{C \stackrel{* {\leftarrow \overline{\mathscr{C \stackrel{* {\to \mathscr{C '$, there exists $\mathscr{C \in \text{CAD (S)$ such that $\mathscr{C \stackrel{* {\to \mathscr{C \stackrel{* {\leftarrow \mathscr{C '$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-260: It is locally confluent if for all $\mathscr{C , \overline{\mathscr{C , \mathscr{C ' \in \text{CAD (S)$ such that $\mathscr{C \stackrel{ {\leftarrow \overline{\mathscr{C \stackrel{ {\to \mathscr{C '$, there exists $\mathscr{C \in \text{CAD (S) : \mathscr{C \stackrel{* {\to \mathscr{C \stackrel{* {\leftarrow \mathscr{C '$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-261: Observing that there exists no infinite chain of reductions in $\text{CAD (S)$ and applying Newman's Lemma (\cite[Lemma 2.7.2]{TRaAT ) we obtain the following result.}}
elements.sent-2411-262: The rewriting system $\text{CAD (S)$ is globally confluent if and only if it is locally confluent.}
elements.sent-2411-263: In view of this lemma, we say that $\text{CAD (S)$ is confluent if it is globally confluent or locally confluent.}
elements.sent-2411-264: We can now state and prove the main result of this section which is reminiscent of the characterization of Gröbner bases by means of confluence (see \cite[Chapter~8]{TRaAT ).}
elements.sent-2411-265: There exists a minimum in $\text{CAD (S)$ if and only if the reduction system $\text{CAD (S)$ is confluent.}}
elements.sent-2411-267: For every $\mathscr{C ,\overline{\mathscr{C }$ and $\mathscr{C ' \in \text{CAD (S)$ such that $\mathscr{C \leftarrow \overline{\mathscr{C \to \mathscr{C '$, $\mathcal{M}$ is smaller than or equal to $\mathscr{C}$ and $\mathscr{C '$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-268: Using Theorem \ref{prop:lien-red-ordre , this is equivalent to $\mathscr{C \stackrel{* {\to \mathcal{M \stackrel{* {\leftarrow \mathscr{C '$, so $\text{CAD (S)$ is confluent.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-269: We assume now that $\text{CAD (S)$ is confluent and show that it has a minimum.}
elements.sent-2411-270: By Proposition \ref{prop:uniqueMin , it is sufficient to show that there is at most one minimal element.}
elements.sent-2411-271: If $\mathscr{C}$ and $\mathscr{C '$ are two minimal elements of $\text{CAD (S)$ we obtain a common finer CAD $\overline{\mathscr{C }$ of $\mathscr{C}$ and $\mathscr{C '$ by building a CAD adapted to all the cells of $\mathscr{C}$ and all the cells of $\mathscr{C '$ (see Remark \ref{rem:Collins ).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-272: Hence, we have $\mathscr{C \stackrel{* {\leftarrow \overline{\mathscr{C \stackrel{* {\to \mathscr{C .$ By confluence there exists $\mathscr{C \in \text{CAD (S) : \mathscr{C \stackrel{* {\to \mathscr{C \stackrel{* {\leftarrow \mathscr{C '$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-273: By minimality of $\mathscr{C}$ and $\mathscr{C '$, we have $\mathscr{C =\mathscr{C = \mathscr{C '$, as requested.}}}}
